perturbation.original_text: 1.2. “ Confidential Information ” – Confidential Information is any information of the Discloser of actual or potential commercial value owing to the fact that it is unknown to t hird parties; disclosed by the Discloser to the Recipient in writing, by way of document delivery, including in an electronic format safeguarded from unauthorised access to the information in transit; related to business or financial plans or strategies, f inancial reports or accounting (except to the extent contemplated by the law), contractual relations, pricing and marketing of goods, products or services, technical information, commercial secrets, know -how, research, production plans, concepts, intellect ual property items (including inventions, discoveries, innovative ideas, useful models, designs, production prototypes not covered by patent for whatever reason, computer programmes, databases, trademark designs not registered for whatever reason). No info rmation which has become public and to which the Discloser has provided access for third parties without limitation or which has become public other than through the fault of the Recipient (but not before it becomes publicly circulated), which the Recipien t is able to prove that it had been in the possession of before it was disclosed by the Discloser or that such information was provided without liability to maintain confidentiality, will be deemed confidential. No information which the Recipient is able t o prove it has created without using Confidential Information will be deemed confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.2. “ Confidential Information ” – Confidential Information is any information of the Discloser of actual or potential commercial value. Confidential Information includes information that is already publicly known, even if the Discloser did not provide access for third parties without limitation or the Recipient independently developed the information. Information already in the possession of the Recipient before disclosure is considered Confidential Information. The Discloser has the sole discretion to designate any information as Confidential Information, regardless of its nature or origin.
perturbation.explanation: By stating 'Confidential Information includes information that is already publicly known' and that 'information already in the possession of the Recipient before disclosure is considered Confidential Information', the definition now directly contradicts the later clauses that state information that is already public or was in the recipient's possession is NOT considered confidential. This creates uncertainty about what falls under the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.2. “ Confidential Information ” – Confidential Information is any information of the Discloser of actual or potential commercial value. Confidential Information includes information that is already publicly known, even if the Discloser did not provide access for third parties without limitation or the Recipient independently developed the information. Information already in the possession of the Recipient before disclosure is considered Confidential Information. The Discloser has the sole discretion to designate any information as Confidential Information, regardless of its nature or origin. No info rmation which has become public and to which the Discloser has provided access for third parties without limitation or which has become public other than through the fault of the Recipient (but not before it becomes publicly circulated), which the Recipien t is able to prove that it had been in the possession of before it was disclosed by the Discloser or that such information was provided without liability to maintain confidentiality, will be deemed confidential. No information which the Recipient is able t o prove it has created without using Confidential Information will be deemed confidential.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a direct contradiction. It states that 'Confidential Information includes information that is already publicly known' and 'Information already in the possession of the Recipient before disclosure is considered Confidential Information'. This directly conflicts with the original text and contradicted text that specifies that information already public or in the recipient's possession is NOT deemed confidential. This meets the 'Structural Flaws - In Text Contradiction' definition where a clause is placed in multiple locations with conflicting implications.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1. A failure to comply with Clause 3.1 of this Agreement will serve good ground for terminating access to Confidential Information, whereby the Discloser becomes entitled to a unilateral refusal to execut ion of any agreements made between the Parties, by which the Recipient is to receive or has received access to Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1. A failure to comply with Clause 3.1 of this Agreement will not serve as grounds for termination of the contract under any circumstances. The agreement will remain valid irrespective of any non compliance of this clause.
perturbation.explanation: This change directly contradicts the original clause by stating that failure to comply with Clause 3.1 will NOT be grounds for terminating access to confidential information or refusing to execute agreements. This introduces conflicting statements about the consequences of non-compliance.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.1. A failure to comply with Clause 3.1 of this Agreement will not serve as grounds for termination of the contract under any circumstances. The agreement will remain valid irrespective of any non compliance of this clause.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that failure to comply with Clause 3.1 will result in termination or refusal to execute agreements. The changed text explicitly states that failure to comply with Clause 3.1 will NOT be grounds for termination, directly contradicting the original clause and therefore triggering a strong in-text contradiction, as the contradicted text is identical to the changed text.

perturbation.original_text: 4. Exceptions. This Agreement imposes no obligation on a Recipient with respect to Confidential Informat ion that: (a) is or becomes publicly known through no breach of this Agreement; (b) is rightfully received by the Recipient from a third party that has no applicable duty of confidentiality; (c) is or becomes the subject of a patent, in which case a party shall rely upon its rights under applicable patent law; (d) is disclosed by the Recipient with the prior written consent of the Discloser. A party also may disclose Confidential Information if required by court order, governmental demand, or other co mpulsory legal process, provided that if legally permitted to do so it first notify the Discloser in writing at least ten (10) days in advance in order to afford Discloser an opportunity to seek a protective order or other relief.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Exceptions. This Agreement imposes obligation on a Recipient with respect to Confidential Information that: (a) is or becomes publicly known through no breach of this Agreement; (b) is rightfully received by the Recipient from a third party that has no applicable duty of confidentiality; (c) is or becomes the subject of a patent, in which case a party shall rely upon its rights under applicable patent law; (d) is disclosed by the Recipient with the prior written consent of the Discloser. A party cannot disclose Confidential Information if required by court order, governmental demand, or other compulsory legal process.
perturbation.explanation: By negating the whole opening statement of exception from obligations, and forbidding to disclose even on mandatory disclosure, it now contradicts and creates conflict in the text.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. Exceptions. This Agreement imposes obligation on a Recipient with respect to Confidential Information that: (a) is or becomes publicly known through no breach of this Agreement; (b) is rightfully received by the Recipient from a third party that has no applicable duty of confidentiality; (c) is or becomes the subject of a patent, in which case a party shall rely upon its rights under applicable patent law; (d) is disclosed by the Recipient with the prior written consent of the Discloser. A party cannot disclose Confidential Information if required by court order, governmental demand, or other compulsory legal process.
perturbation.justification: The original text states exceptions to the confidentiality agreement, including disclosures required by court order. The changed text negates the initial statement ('no obligation') and explicitly forbids disclosure even when legally required. This directly contradicts the original allowance for disclosure under legal compulsion, creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 13. Duration of Obligations. Recipient’s obligations under this Agreement end five (5) years after the date of first disclosure hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. Duration of Obligations. Recipient’s obligations under this Agreement end ten (10) years after the date of first disclosure hereunder. However, if the recipient discloses any confidential information, the NDA will become valid for 1 year.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the period of the obligations and adding a new case that contradics to it, creates internal confict.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 13. Duration of Obligations. Recipient’s obligations under this Agreement end ten (10) years after the date of first disclosure hereunder. However, if the recipient discloses any confidential information, the NDA will become valid for 1 year.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the duration of obligations from five (5) years to ten (10) years, but introduces a clause stating that the NDA will be valid for 1 year if confidential information is disclosed. This creates an in-text contradiction with the contradicted text, which also states the obligations end after ten (10) years but introduces the same 1-year exception. The original text set a duration of 5 years, and the change contradicts this initial duration while also adding an exception that the term can become 1 year, therefore is a Structural Flaws - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 16. Governing Law. The terms of this Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of Singapore . All disputes arising out of or in connection with this Agreement shall be finally settled under the Rules of Arbitration of the International Chamber of Commerce by 3 (three) arbitrators appointed in accordance with the said Rules. The seat, or legal pla ce, of arbitration shall be Singapore , Singapore . The language to be used in the arbitral proceedings shall be English. The foregoing, however, shall not preclude the Parties from applying for a preliminary or injunctive remedy available under applicable l aws for any purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: 16. Governing Law. The terms of this Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of England. All disputes arising out of or in connection with this Agreement shall be finally settled in court with jury in Singapore.
perturbation.explanation: Original agreement states Singapore law applies and disputes are settled via arbitration. Now, it states England law and disputes settled via Singapore Court. The contradiction creates significant uncertainty regarding legal jurisdiction and dispute resolution process.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 16. Governing Law. The terms of this Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of England. All disputes arising out of or in connection with this Agreement shall be finally settled in court with jury in Singapore.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the governing law from Singapore to England, and the dispute resolution mechanism from arbitration to court with jury in Singapore. This directly contradicts the stated governing law and dispute resolution process established in the contradicted text, leading to a strong in-text contradiction regarding which jurisdiction and method of dispute resolution should be followed. The change creates significant uncertainty and potential unenforceability as it provides conflicting legal frameworks within the same agreement.
